title: The Switch That Must Not Surprise Anyone
context: A launch-readiness steering meeting has been called for the new Expenses module. The VP of Revenue wants conference buzz in six weeks. The Product Manager wants a smooth customer story. The Payroll Director is worried about side effects in the live payroll experience, and Customer Success wants a rollout they can explain without caveats. You are the architect in the room, and everyone is looking for a direction that feels commercially ambitious without creating hidden coupling.

questions.prompt: The VP of Revenue opens: "Show the Expenses tab to every admin account now, grey it out for customers who have not bought it, and let Sales use the clicks as pipeline data." How do you respond?

options.text: We can do that and treat the product navigation as a marketing surface. A greyed-out tab keeps the module visible, gives Revenue a clean upsell story, and lets us measure interest before the launch lands. We can work through any entitlement edge cases during delivery. It also gives Revenue a bolder launch narrative and postpones the awkward entitlement questions until after the conference window.
options.explanation: The commercial instinct is understandable in the room. This is failure mode A — over-agreement: it accepts the proposal before testing whether live navigation should represent entitlement, discovery, or both. Mixing those concerns creates avoidable confusion for admins and support teams.

options.text: I would keep entitlement authoritative in the live product and solve demand generation separately. If Revenue wants visibility, we can use release messaging, a marketing page, or admin notifications, but the navigation should reflect what the customer has bought and can use.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the framing pattern: separate the problem before choosing the solution. The answer distinguishes commercial discovery from entitlement control and names the simpler option that preserves a clean operating boundary.

options.text: We should not do that because it will confuse customers and create unnecessary questions for support. The safer answer is to leave the module hidden until every commercial and product detail is perfect.
options.explanation: The concern about confusion is reasonable. This is failure mode B — correct instinct, wrong execution: it pushes back without offering a framework or an alternative path for Revenue's underlying goal, so the architect sounds defensive rather than useful.

questions.prompt: The Engineering Manager says: "Claims already has a switch, so the fastest route is to reuse it, relabel the navigation, and sort the model separation out later." What do you say?

options.text: That is probably the quickest implementation, so we should take it. Users already associate Claims with spending, and reusing the switch means less engineering effort now. We can unwind the coupling after launch if customers push back. It also gives Revenue a bolder launch narrative and postpones the awkward entitlement questions until after the conference window.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. The delivery pressure is real. This is failure mode D — scope explosion through hidden coupling: it trades a short-term shortcut for a muddled domain boundary and assumes the cleanup will be cheap later.

options.text: I would keep Claims and Expenses as separate entitlements even if we later present them through a coordinated experience. The question is not only navigation speed; it is whether purchase state, visibility, support ownership, and downstream behaviour stay clear when one module changes and the other does not.
options.explanation: This demonstrates naming the domain and forcing the room to justify the heavier option. The answer separates presentation from entitlement and anchors the decision on ownership and change impact rather than implementation convenience alone.

options.text: Let me take that away and map every screen, flag, and customer journey before we decide. Once we have a fuller dependency inventory, we can come back with a recommendation on whether one switch or two is cleaner.
options.explanation: The wish to be thorough is understandable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: there is already enough information to frame the decision in the room by separating entitlement from presentation before commissioning more analysis.

questions.prompt: Customer Success adds: "Several design partners want to pilot Expenses in one department before going company-wide. Can we make activation department-scoped for phase one?" How do you handle it?

options.text: Yes, and we should probably go further and allow activation by department, legal entity, and manager hierarchy from day one. That gives Sales maximum flexibility and avoids another round of architecture work when more rollout patterns appear. It also gives Revenue a bolder launch narrative and postpones the awkward entitlement questions until after the conference window.
options.explanation: The desire to support pilots is understandable. This is failure mode D — scope explosion: it turns one rollout request into a broad access model without first testing whether the business need truly requires that complexity.

options.text: I would separate pilot control from entitlement shape. If the requirement is only to manage exposure during rollout, we can use customer-level activation with internal feature controls or managed onboarding. We should only introduce department-scoped entitlement if there is a durable business need that affects policy, reporting, or support boundaries after launch.
options.explanation: This demonstrates resisting scope creep and naming the simpler option. The answer keeps the room focused on the actual requirement — controlled rollout — instead of accepting a permanently heavier entitlement model by reflex.

options.text: Department-scoped activation is too messy, so I would rule it out now. Pilots create edge cases, and phase one should tolerate no exceptions if we want to stay on schedule.
options.explanation: The instinct to protect delivery is reasonable. This is failure mode B — correct instinct, wrong execution: it rejects the request too bluntly and misses the chance to offer a lighter mechanism that could satisfy the rollout concern.

questions.prompt: The Product Manager says: "Marketing needs something customer-visible this quarter. Can we release the switch and an empty Expenses shell first, then fill the workflows in later?" What is your response?

options.text: We can release the shell early, tell customers the experience is evolving, and use the live product to build momentum. Seeing the module in place will reassure the market that the launch is real, even if the operational detail lands in later increments.
options.explanation: The pressure to show progress is understandable. This is failure mode A — over-agreement: it accepts a visible product state that weakens trust without first translating what Marketing really needs from the quarter-end milestone.

options.text: I would translate that ask into a requirement for credible launch evidence rather than a literal empty shell. Internal previews, controlled demos, or customer-safe beta access could satisfy the date, but a visible module with no working outcome weakens trust and creates support noise.
options.explanation: This demonstrates translating a business slogan into a requirement. The answer reframes 'something customer-visible' into the underlying need for launch confidence and then offers lighter options that do not damage the product contract.

options.text: We need a fuller launch workback before answering that. If Marketing, Support, Revenue, and Product each document their threshold for a credible release, we can decide later whether the shell is acceptable.
options.explanation: The coordination instinct is reasonable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: the architect can already frame the decision by separating proof of progress from customer-visible emptiness instead of pushing the question into another meeting.

questions.prompt: The room looks to you to close. Which summary best moves the meeting forward?

options.text: We have agreed to keep the live navigation aligned to bought entitlement, keep Claims and Expenses as separate module controls, treat pilot rollout as a lighter exposure problem unless a durable sub-customer entitlement need appears, and meet the quarter milestone with controlled evidence rather than an empty shell. Product and Revenue can now shape the go-to-market plan inside those boundaries.
options.explanation: This demonstrates meeting closure: it summarises the decision, the criteria behind it, and the bounded follow-up. A good architect closes by confirming what was decided and what did not get coupled into scope.

options.text: The safest next step is for each function to return with more detailed concerns and proposed mitigations, because there are still too many moving parts for a clean decision today. We can use that material to decide the entitlement and launch shape in the next steering session.
options.explanation: The wish to reduce risk is understandable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: the room already has enough clarity to decide the boundary conditions, so reopening the same debate loses momentum and ownership.

options.text: We should proceed with the broadest design so we do not corner ourselves later: universal visibility, flexible scoped rollout, and a shared switch model. That keeps every commercial path open while engineering works through the details.
options.explanation: The desire to preserve optionality is understandable. This is failure mode D — scope explosion: it bundles several heavier decisions together in the name of flexibility and turns a focused launch discussion into a platform design problem.
